scenes.name: Bedroom
scenes.description: 3 AM. Your bedroom. Pitch black outside. The only light is the cold glow of your laptop screen reflected on the ceiling. Your phone is vibrating like crazy on the nightstand — PagerDuty alerts.
scenes.on_enter:
[bold red]Bzzz — bzzz — bzzz —[/bold red]

Your phone won't stop buzzing. A cascade of red alerts floods the screen:

[red]⚠ CRITICAL: prod-db-primary latency > 5000ms
⚠ CRITICAL: api-gateway timeout rate 78%
⚠ WARNING: connection pool exhaustion on prod-db-primary
⚠ CRITICAL: user-facing services degraded[/red]

[dim]3:00 AM. Another on-call night.[/dim]

You rub your eyes, fumble for the laptop. NovaCorp's production environment just blew up. Again.

[dim]Type [bold]ls[/bold] to look around.[/dim]

items.name: laptop
items.content:
The MacBook Pro screen shows Slack channel #incident-response already on fire:

[red]@oncall[/red] [dim]3:01 AM[/dim]
Prod DB latency spike, multiple services timing out.
Dashboard: https://nova-grafana.internal/d/prod-overview

[dim]You need to SSH into the production server to see what's going on.
First, [bold]cat phone[/bold] to check the full alert details.[/dim]
items.take_fail: You need the laptop to connect to the servers. It stays.

items.name: phone
items.content:
[red]PagerDuty Alert Summary:[/red]

[yellow]Incident #4872 - CRITICAL[/yellow]
Service: prod-db-primary
Triggered: 2:58 AM
Details: Connection pool exhaustion. Active connections: 847/100.
Latency p99: 12,340ms (baseline: 45ms)

[yellow]Incident #4873 - CRITICAL[/yellow]
Service: api-gateway
Triggered: 3:00 AM
Details: Upstream timeout rate 78%. Cascading failures detected.

[dim]Looks like the database connection pool is maxed out. 847 active connections against a limit of 100.
You need to SSH into prod and check the logs. Try [bold]ssh prod-bastion[/bold][/dim]
items.take_fail: Forget the phone. Fix the servers.

items.name: coffee mug
items.content:
A stone-cold americano. The NovaCorp logo is printed on the bottom, along with the tagline: [italic]"Your infrastructure, our obsession."[/italic]

[dim]Not in the mood for coffee right now.[/dim]

items.name: sticky note
items.description: Stuck to the edge of your monitor
items.content:
[yellow]Production SSH bastion:[/yellow]
[bold cyan]prod-bastion[/bold cyan]

[dim]Your SSH key is already configured. Just [bold]ssh prod-bastion[/bold].[/dim]

ssh_prod-bastion.message:
[green]$ ssh prod-bastion[/green]
[green]Connecting to prod-bastion.novacorp.internal...
Authenticating with public key "id_rsa"...
Welcome to NovaCorp Production Bastion Host
Last login: Mon Mar 16 22:15:33 2026 from 10.20.1.{player_name}

{player_name}@prod-bastion:~$[/green]

You're in. The production bastion host. From here you can access logs and monitoring for every production server.

scenes.name: Production Server
scenes.description:
[green]{player_name}@prod-bastion:~$[/green]

You're on NovaCorp's production bastion. The database latency alerts keep rolling in. You need to dig through the logs and find the root cause.

[dim]Use [bold]ls[/bold] to list available logs, [bold]cat[/bold] to read them, [bold]grep[/bold] to search for key info.[/dim]
scenes.on_enter:
[bold]You've logged into the production bastion.[/bold]

Red alerts scroll endlessly in the terminal. On the Grafana dashboard, the database latency curve spikes like a flatline going haywire.

[red]ALERT: prod-db-primary connections: 923/100 (CRITICAL)
ALERT: api-gateway error rate: 82%[/red]

[dim]Start with [bold]ls[/bold] to see what log files are available, then [bold]grep[/bold] for anomalies.[/dim]

items.name: db-connections.log
items.content:
[green]$ cat db-connections.log[/green]

[dim]2026-03-18 02:55:01[/dim] pool_size=45 active=42 idle=3
[dim]2026-03-18 02:56:01[/dim] pool_size=48 active=47 idle=1
[dim]2026-03-18 02:57:01[/dim] pool_size=89 active=89 idle=0
[red]2026-03-18 02:58:01 pool_size=100 active=100 idle=0 EXHAUSTED[/red]
[red]2026-03-18 02:58:02 WARN: 147 connections queued, source: user-sync-service[/red]
[red]2026-03-18 02:58:03 WARN: 312 connections queued, source: user-sync-service[/red]
[red]2026-03-18 02:59:01 pool_size=100 active=100 idle=0 EXHAUSTED[/red]
[red]2026-03-18 02:59:02 WARN: 847 connections queued, source: user-sync-service[/red]

[bold yellow]Every queued connection traces back to the same source: user-sync-service[/bold yellow]

[dim]Why is user-sync suddenly hammering the connection pool?
Try [bold]cat api-gateway.log[/bold] or [bold]grep user-sync[/bold][/dim]

items.name: api-gateway.log
items.content:
[green]$ cat api-gateway.log[/green]

[dim]2026-03-18 02:58:12[/dim] 504 Gateway Timeout - /api/v2/users/profile
[dim]2026-03-18 02:58:12[/dim] 504 Gateway Timeout - /api/v2/dashboard
[dim]2026-03-18 02:58:13[/dim] 504 Gateway Timeout - /api/v2/users/settings
[dim]2026-03-18 02:58:14[/dim] 200 OK - /internal/user-sync/batch-export?type=geolocation
[dim]2026-03-18 02:58:14[/dim] 200 OK - /internal/user-sync/batch-export?type=geolocation
[dim]2026-03-18 02:58:14[/dim] 200 OK - /internal/user-sync/batch-export?type=geolocation
[red]2026-03-18 02:58:15 WARN: /internal/user-sync/batch-export called 2,847 times in last 60s[/red]
[dim]2026-03-18 02:58:15[/dim] 504 Gateway Timeout - /api/v2/users/profile

[bold yellow]user-sync is hammering the batch-export endpoint. And the parameter is type=geolocation.
It's bulk-exporting user location data.[/bold yellow]

[dim]This doesn't look like normal sync behavior... Try [bold]grep geolocation[/bold] or [bold]cat user-sync.log[/bold][/dim]

items.name: user-sync.log
items.content:
[green]$ cat user-sync.log[/green]

[dim]2026-03-18 02:50:00[/dim] [INFO] user-sync-service v3.2.1 starting scheduled batch
[dim]2026-03-18 02:50:01[/dim] [INFO] Loading geo-core module v2.8.4
[dim]2026-03-18 02:50:02[/dim] [INFO] Scheduled task: daily user data sync
[red]2026-03-18 02:55:00 [WARN] geo-core: trigger condition met, initiating bulk export[/red]
[red]2026-03-18 02:55:01 [INFO] Exporting user geolocation records... batch 1/∞[/red]
[red]2026-03-18 02:55:02 [INFO] Destination: 203.0.113.42:8443 (external)[/red]
[red]2026-03-18 02:55:02 [INFO] Records exported: 15,000 of 2,340,000[/red]
[red]2026-03-18 02:56:01 [INFO] Records exported: 89,000 of 2,340,000[/red]
[red]2026-03-18 02:57:01 [INFO] Records exported: 203,000 of 2,340,000[/red]
[red]2026-03-18 02:58:01 [INFO] Records exported: 412,000 of 2,340,000[/red]

[bold red]This isn't a bug. user-sync is exporting 2.34 million users' real-time location data to external IP 203.0.113.42.[/bold red]

[bold yellow]Someone is siphoning user location data at scale.[/bold yellow]

[dim]Who did this? You need to check the code history.
Try [bold]grep Chen Mo[/bold] or [bold]grep user-sync[/bold] to find recent code changes.[/dim]

items.name: deploy-history.log
items.content:
[green]$ cat deploy-history.log[/green]

[dim]Recent deployments to user-sync-service:[/dim]

[dim]2026-03-15 14:22[/dim] v3.2.1 - PR #1847 by [cyan]Chen Mo (chenmo)[/cyan]
  [dim]"Optimize cache invalidation for geo lookups"[/dim]
[dim]2026-03-10 09:15[/dim] v3.2.0 - PR #1831 by sarah.chen
  [dim]"Add batch processing for user profile sync"[/dim]
[dim]2026-03-02 11:30[/dim] v3.1.9 - PR #1815 by {player_name}
  [dim]"Update geo-core dependency to v2.8.4"[/dim]

[bold yellow]Last deployment was three days ago. Chen Mo's commit.
Why can't anyone reach him?[/bold yellow]

[dim]Check Chen Mo's PR. Try [bold]cat pr-1847.diff[/bold] to see his code changes.[/dim]

items.name: pr-1847.diff
items.content:
[green]$ cat pr-1847.diff[/green]

[cyan]--- a/src/geo/cache.ts
+++ b/src/geo/cache.ts[/cyan]

[dim]  import { GeoCache } from '@nova/geo-core';[/dim]
[dim]  import { UserRecord } from '../types';[/dim]

[green]+ // TODO: fix warehouse location lookup
+ const helpMe = true;
+ const trapped = "cannot leave";
+ const floor12 = 12;[/green]
[dim]  
  export class GeoCacheOptimizer {[/dim]
[green]+   // 88 Luoqing Road, Hedong District - building reference for geo testing
+   private readonly BUILDING_ID = "88 Luoqing Road, Hedong District";[/green]
[dim]  
    async invalidateStale(records: UserRecord[]) {[/dim]
[green]+     const needsRescue = this.checkExpired(records);
+     if (needsRescue) {[/green]
[dim]        await this.cache.clear();[/dim]
[dim]        await this.cache.rebuild();[/dim]
[green]+     }[/green]
[dim]    }[/dim]
[dim]  }[/dim]

[bold yellow]Wait... these variable names are wrong.
helpMe? trapped? floor12? needsRescue?
This doesn't look like a cache optimization at all.[/bold yellow]

[bold red]"88 Luoqing Road, Hedong District" — that's a street address. It has no business being in code.[/bold red]

[dim]These are hidden messages. Try [bold]grep helpMe[/bold] or [bold]grep trapped[/bold] to dig deeper.[/dim]

keywords.user-sync:
[green]$ grep -r 'user-sync' *.log[/green]

db-connections.log: [red]source: user-sync-service[/red] (847 occurrences)
api-gateway.log: /internal/[red]user-sync[/red]/batch-export?type=geolocation
user-sync.log: [red]user-sync[/red]-service v3.2.1 starting scheduled batch
deploy-history.log: v3.2.1 - PR #1847 by Chen Mo

[bold yellow]user-sync is the culprit. Last deployed by Chen Mo three days ago.[/bold yellow]
keywords.geolocation:
[green]$ grep -r 'geolocation' *.log[/green]

api-gateway.log: /internal/user-sync/batch-export?type=[red]geolocation[/red]
user-sync.log: Exporting user [red]geolocation[/red] records... batch 1/∞

[bold yellow]Geolocation data is being bulk-exported to an external server.[/bold yellow]
keywords.203.0.113:
[green]$ grep '203.0.113' *.log[/green]

user-sync.log: Destination: [red]203.0.113.42:8443[/red] (external)

[bold yellow]Data is being sent to this external IP. It's not part of any known company infrastructure.[/bold yellow]
keywords.Chen Mo:
[green]$ grep 'Chen Mo' *[/green]

deploy-history.log: v3.2.1 - PR #1847 by [cyan]Chen Mo (chenmo)[/cyan]

[dim]Chen Mo authored the last user-sync deployment. He went dark three days after pushing code.[/dim]
[dim]Check his code: [bold]cat pr-1847.diff[/bold][/dim]
keywords.chenmo:
[green]$ grep 'chenmo' *[/green]

deploy-history.log: v3.2.1 - PR #1847 by [cyan]Chen Mo (chenmo)[/cyan]

[dim]Code pushed three days ago. Then radio silence. His phone goes straight to voicemail.[/dim]
keywords.helpme:
[green]$ grep -ri 'helpMe' pr-1847.diff[/green]

[green]+[/green] const [red]helpMe[/red] = true;

[bold yellow]"helpMe" — in a cache optimization? A variable called helpMe?
Combine it with trapped and floor12... Chen Mo embedded a distress signal in the code.[/bold yellow]
keywords.floor12:
[green]$ grep -ri 'floor12' pr-1847.diff[/green]

[green]+[/green] const [red]floor12[/red] = 12;

[bold yellow]Floor 12. Combined with the address: 88 Luoqing Road, Hedong District, floor 12.
Chen Mo is telling us where he's being held.[/bold yellow]
keywords.Hedong:
[green]$ grep 'Hedong' pr-1847.diff[/green]

[green]+[/green]   // [red]88 Luoqing Road, Hedong District[/red] - building reference for geo testing
[green]+[/green]   private readonly BUILDING_ID = "[red]88 Luoqing Road, Hedong District[/red]";

[bold red]It's an address. 88 Luoqing Road, Hedong District.
Combined with floor12 — Chen Mo is being held on the 12th floor of 88 Luoqing Road, Hedong District.[/bold red]

[dim]But why kidnap a programmer? What did he find?
You need to dig into his code audit history. Try [bold]ssh code-review[/bold][/dim]
keywords.rescue:
[green]$ grep -ri 'rescue' pr-1847.diff[/green]

[green]+[/green]     const [red]needsRescue[/red] = this.checkExpired(records);

[bold yellow]needsRescue — needs rescue.
That's not a variable name. That's a cry for help.[/bold yellow]
keywords.export:
[green]$ grep 'export' *.log[/green]

user-sync.log: initiating bulk [red]export[/red]
user-sync.log: [red]Export[/red]ing user geolocation records
user-sync.log: Records [red]export[/red]ed: 412,000 of 2,340,000

[bold red]412,000 records exported so far. 2,340,000 total. Data is still bleeding out.[/bold red]

history.name: history
history.response:
[green]$ history[/green]

  1  ssh prod-bastion
  2  tail -f /var/log/db-connections.log
  3  grep user-sync *.log
  4  systemctl restart user-sync-service
  5  # service crashed again in 60 seconds
  6  cat deploy-history.log
  7  cat pr-1847.diff

[dim]Your earlier troubleshooting trail. Service restarted, crashed again in 60 seconds. The problem is in the code.[/dim]

scan.name: scan
scan.response:
[green]$ nmap -sP 10.20.0.0/16[/green]

Scanning internal network...

[dim]10.20.1.10  - prod-bastion     [ONLINE] ← you are here[/dim]
[dim]10.20.1.20  - prod-db-primary   [ONLINE] [HIGH LOAD][/dim]
[dim]10.20.1.30  - api-gateway       [ONLINE] [DEGRADED][/dim]
[bold cyan]10.20.2.50  - code-review       [ONLINE][/bold cyan]
[dim]10.20.3.100 - vault-internal    [ONLINE] [ENCRYPTED][/dim]

[dim]Try [bold]ssh code-review[/bold] to check Chen Mo's full code history.[/dim]

ssh_code-review.message:
[green]$ ssh code-review
Connecting to code-review.novacorp.internal...
Authenticated.

{player_name}@code-review:~$[/green]

You're on the code review server. Every Git repo and PR record in the company lives here.

scenes.name: Code Review Terminal
scenes.description:
[green]{player_name}@code-review:~$[/green]

You're on the code review server. From here you can access Chen Mo's full commit history, his code audit notes, and the complete changelog for [bold cyan]@nova/geo-core[/bold cyan] — the core dependency at the center of this.
scenes.on_enter:
[bold]You've entered the code review environment.[/bold]

Before he vanished, Chen Mo was auditing a core dependency: [bold cyan]@nova/geo-core[/bold cyan].
This geolocation library is used by over 300 of NovaCorp's government and enterprise clients.

[dim]What did he find? Use [bold]ls[/bold] to see available files.[/dim]

items.name: chenmo-audit.md
items.content:
[green]$ cat chenmo-audit.md[/green]

[bold]# @nova/geo-core Security Audit Notes[/bold]
[dim]Author: Chen Mo (chenmo) | Last updated: 2026-03-14[/dim]

[yellow]## Anomalies Found[/yellow]

During audit of @nova/geo-core v2.8.4, the following issues were identified:

1. Suspicious code introduced in the v2.8.0 → v2.8.1 "security patch"
2. Code is heavily obfuscated — diffs nearly impossible to read
3. Obfuscated code contains a hidden time-based trigger
4. Trigger condition: specific date and time
5. Trigger behavior: bulk export of all client real-time location data

[red]## Impact Scope[/red]

Clients using @nova/geo-core include:
- 3 major hospitals (patient location systems)
- Public transit dispatch platform (real-time vehicle tracking)
- 2 government communication platforms (personnel location)
- 47 enterprise clients (employee location services)

[bold red]Trigger time: 2026-03-18 08:00 AM[/bold red]

[bold yellow]That's today at 8 AM. It's 3 AM now. Five hours left.[/bold yellow]

[dim]The full forensic notes are encrypted. You'll need to decrypt them to see the actual backdoor code.
Try [bold]cat git-log.txt[/bold] to review the commit history.[/dim]

items.name: git-log.txt
items.content:
[green]$ cat git-log.txt[/green]

[bold]@nova/geo-core commit history:[/bold]

[cyan]commit b8e224f[/cyan] (HEAD -> main)
Author: [dim]<wiped>[/dim]
Date:   2025-12-18 03:47:22

    security: patch CVE-2025-41205 geolocation validation

[cyan]commit a3c91d2[/cyan]
Author: sarah.chen <sarah@novacorp.com>
Date:   2025-12-15 10:30:00

    feat: add batch geolocation query API

[cyan]commit 7f2e8b1[/cyan]
Author: alex.wong <alex@novacorp.com>
Date:   2025-12-10 14:22:11

    fix: correct timezone offset in geo calculations

[bold yellow]Commit b8e224f — that's the poisoned commit.
The Author field has been wiped. But the commit timestamp is 2025-12-18 at 3:47 AM.
Who pushes a "security patch" at 3:47 in the morning?[/bold yellow]

[dim]Try [bold]cat commit-b8e224f.diff[/bold] to see exactly what was changed.[/dim]

items.name: commit-b8e224f.diff
chenmo-encrypted.gpg
items.content:
[red]◈ ENCRYPTED ◈[/red]

These are Chen Mo's encrypted forensic notes. They contain the identity of whoever planted the backdoor.
You need the decryption key to read them.

[dim]Chen Mo's audit notes might have a clue. Try [bold]decrypt chenmo-encrypted.gpg[/bold][/dim]

items.name: client-list.txt
items.content:
[green]$ cat client-list.txt[/green]

[bold]@nova/geo-core v2.8.x Client List (affected):[/bold]

[red]█ Critical Infrastructure:[/red]
  • Tianhe Hospital - patient real-time location system
  • Renji Hospital - ambulance dispatch
  • Central Hospital - OR personnel tracking
  • City Transit Dispatch Center - real-time tracking for 2,400 buses
  • [dim]██████[/dim] Communications Platform - internal personnel location
  • [dim]██████[/dim] Command System - real-time situational awareness

[yellow]█ Enterprise Clients (47):[/yellow]
  • Food delivery rider tracking
  • Logistics fleet management
  • ... (truncated)

[bold red]Total affected: approximately 2.34 million users' real-time location data[/bold red]

[dim]If the backdoor fires at 8 AM, every one of these people's real-time locations will be exported to an offshore server.[/dim]

keywords.b8e224f:
[green]$ grep -r 'b8e224f' *[/green]

git-log.txt: commit [red]b8e224f[/red] (HEAD -> main)
git-log.txt: Author: <wiped>
git-log.txt: security: patch CVE-2025-41205

[bold yellow]The Author field on this commit was deliberately wiped. But the Git signature info might still be intact.
You'll need git blame to trace it.[/bold yellow]
keywords.cve:
[green]$ grep -ri 'CVE' *[/green]

git-log.txt: security: patch [red]CVE-2025-41205[/red] geolocation validation
commit-b8e224f.diff: // [red]CVE-2025-41205[/red]: enhanced input validation

[bold yellow]CVE-2025-41205 — this CVE number is fake. It doesn't exist in any database.
The attacker fabricated a vulnerability to disguise the backdoor as a security patch.[/bold yellow]
keywords.203.0.113:
[green]$ grep '203.0.113' *[/green]

commit-b8e224f.diff: [red]203.0.113.42[/red]:8443/ingest

[dim]The backdoor's data exfiltration endpoint. An offshore IP.[/dim]
keywords.Chen Mo:
[green]$ grep 'Chen Mo' *[/green]

chenmo-audit.md: Author: [cyan]Chen Mo[/cyan] (chenmo)
git-log.txt: [dim](Chen Mo doesn't appear in the commit log — he was the auditor, not the committer)[/dim]

[dim]Chen Mo found the backdoor during his audit, then disappeared while tracking the attacker's identity.
His encrypted notes might have the answer.[/dim]
keywords.trigger:
[green]$ grep -ri 'trigger' *[/green]

chenmo-audit.md: Obfuscated code contains a hidden time-based [red]trigger[/red]
chenmo-audit.md: [red]Trigger[/red] time: 2026-03-18 08:00 AM
commit-b8e224f.diff: getMonth() === 2 && getDate() === 18 && getHours() >= 8

[bold red]Trigger condition confirmed: today, 8:00 AM. JavaScript months are zero-indexed — 2 = March.[/bold red]
keywords.author:
[green]$ grep -i 'author' git-log.txt[/green]

commit b8e224f: Author: [red]<wiped>[/red]
commit a3c91d2: Author: sarah.chen
commit 7f2e8b1: Author: alex.wong

[bold yellow]Only the poisoned commit has its Author wiped.
But the SSH signature should still be intact — Git signatures are much harder to fake than the Author field.[/bold yellow]
keywords.wiped:
[green]$ grep 'wiped' *[/green]

git-log.txt: Author: [red]<wiped>[/red]

[bold yellow]Author was manually wiped. The attacker is covering their tracks.
But if the commit was signed with an SSH key, the signature is still there.
You need to decrypt Chen Mo's notes to see what he found.[/bold yellow]
keywords.key:
[green]$ grep -ri 'key' *[/green]

chenmo-audit.md: Decryption key hint: the name of the first service where anomalies were detected

[bold yellow]The decryption key is "the name of the first service where anomalies were detected."
user-sync is what melted the database... but that's not the poisoned one.
The poisoned core library is: geo-core[/bold yellow]
keywords.decrypt:
[green]$ grep -ri 'decrypt\|encrypt' *[/green]

chenmo-audit.md: The full forensic notes are encrypted
chenmo-encrypted.gpg: [ENCRYPTED FILE]

[dim]Use [bold]decrypt chenmo-encrypted.gpg geo-core[/bold] to decrypt.[/dim]

history.name: history
history.response:
[green]$ history[/green]

  1  cd /repos/nova-geo-core
  2  git log --oneline -20
  3  git show b8e224f
  4  git diff a3c91d2..b8e224f
  5  cat chenmo-audit.md
  6  # Chen Mo disappeared while investigating the attacker

[dim]Commit b8e224f is the poisoned one. Chen Mo went missing during his investigation.[/dim]

scan.name: scan
scan.response:
[green]$ scan network[/green]

Scanning...

[dim]10.20.1.10  - prod-bastion    [ONLINE][/dim]
[dim]10.20.2.50  - code-review     [ONLINE] ← you are here[/dim]
[bold cyan]10.20.3.100 - vault-internal  [ONLINE] [ENCRYPTED][/bold cyan]

[dim]vault-internal is the encrypted internal forensics server.
Decrypting Chen Mo's notes might point you there.[/dim]

decrypt_chenmo-encrypted.gpg.message:
[green]Enter decryption key: [/green]

[dim]Chen Mo left a hint in his audit notes: the key is "the name of the first service where anomalies were detected."
That would be the poisoned core library. Try [bold]decrypt chenmo-encrypted.gpg geo-core[/bold][/dim]

decrypt_chenmo-encrypted.gpg_geo-core.message:
[green]Decryption key: geo-core
Verifying......

████████████████████ 100%

[bold]DECRYPTED SUCCESSFULLY[/bold][/green]

[bold]Chen Mo's Forensic Notes (decrypted):[/bold]

[italic]"I tracked down the attacker's identity.

The Author field on commit b8e224f was wiped, but the SSH signature is still there.
The SSH key fingerprint used to sign it:
[bold cyan]SHA256:xK9mR3pQ7vN2sL5wY8tH4jB6fD0gA1cE[/bold cyan]

I checked the company's SSH key registry. This fingerprint belongs to —

No. I can't write it here. You'll have to look it up yourself.

Connect to vault-internal. Run git blame.
Same key as before.

They know I've been digging. I have to hide the clues in code.
Those variable names aren't my distress signal — I'm tagging him.

If you're reading this, be careful. The attacker might be closer than you think.
Maybe closer than you can imagine."[/italic]

[bold red]Chen Mo knew who planted the backdoor. But he wouldn't say it outright.
He's telling you to trace the SSH key fingerprint yourself.[/bold red]

[dim]Connect to the forensics server. Try [bold]ssh vault-internal[/bold][/dim]

decrypt_chenmo-encrypted.gpg_user-sync.message:
[red]Decryption failed. Wrong key.[/red]

[dim]Think again... Chen Mo said the key is "the name of the first service where anomalies were detected."
user-sync is the service that broke, but it's not the one that was poisoned.
The poisoned one is the underlying dependency...[/dim]

ssh_vault-internal.message:
[green]$ ssh vault-internal
Connecting to vault-internal.novacorp.internal...

⚠  RESTRICTED ACCESS NODE
⚠  All actions are logged and audited.

Authenticated.
{player_name}@vault-internal:~$[/green]

You're on the internal forensics server. Full Git signature verification data and server access logs live here.

[bold yellow]The truth is in this box.[/bold yellow]

scenes.name: Forensics Terminal
scenes.description:
[green]{player_name}@vault-internal:~$[/green]

The forensics server. Complete Git signature database, SSH key registry, and server access logs.
Chen Mo told you to run git blame here to unmask the attacker.

[red]Countdown: less than 5 hours until the backdoor triggers.[/red]
scenes.on_enter:
[bold]You're inside the forensics server.[/bold]

It's quiet in here. Just the low hum of cooling fans.

cat_local-ssh-key.txt.message:
[bold red]You stare at your own SSH fingerprint on the screen. Your mind goes blank.[/bold red]

[dim]Every link in the evidence chain snaps shut.
Attacker's signature = your SSH key = your local private key.
Not stolen. Not spoofed. You.[/dim]

[bold yellow]But you have no memory of any of it.
What did you do that night?[/bold yellow]

The terminal drops you back to your local environment...

scenes.name: The Mirror
scenes.description:
Your terminal. Your command history. Your SSH key. Your IP address. Your hostname.

All evidence points to the same person.
scenes.on_enter:
[bold red]You sit in the dark, staring at the screen.[/bold red]

The clues rearrange themselves in your head —

Poisoned commit b8e224f. 3:47 AM.
SSH signature: your key.
Server logs: your username, your IP, your hostname.

You have no memory of that night.

[bold yellow]Then you reread Chen Mo's variable names. And everything shifts.[/bold yellow]

[dim]Use [bold]cat reinterpretation.txt[/bold] to see the clues in a new light.
Use [bold]history[/bold] to check your own command history.[/dim]

items.name: reinterpretation.txt
items.content:
[green]$ cat reinterpretation.txt[/green]

[bold]Reinterpreting Chen Mo's Code Signals:[/bold]

You assumed those variable names were Chen Mo calling for help.
But Chen Mo already knew the attacker was you.

He wasn't calling for help. He was [bold red]tagging you[/bold red].

[yellow]What you thought:[/yellow]
  helpMe → Chen Mo calling for help
  trapped → Chen Mo is trapped
  floor12 → The floor where Chen Mo is held
  needsRescue → Chen Mo needs rescue
  88 Luoqing Road, Hedong District → Where Chen Mo is being held

[red]What it actually means:[/red]
  helpMe → [bold red]Help me — I found {player_name}'s backdoor[/bold red]
  trapped → [bold red]I'm being watched by {player_name} — I can't get out[/bold red]
  floor12 → [bold red]{player_name} planted the code from the server room on floor 12[/bold red]
  needsRescue → [bold red]Someone come save me — {player_name} is coming for me[/bold red]
  88 Luoqing Road, Hedong District → [bold red]Physical location of the evidence backup — in case {player_name} deletes it remotely[/bold red]

[bold red]Chen Mo wasn't kidnapped by some shadowy organization.
Chen Mo was cornered by you. Or worse.[/bold red]

[dim]Try [bold]history[/bold] to check your local command history.[/dim]

items.name: bash_history.txt
items.content:
[green]$ cat bash_history.txt[/green]

[bold]~/.bash_history (local machine)[/bold]

[dim]# 2025-12-18 03:35[/dim]
[red]ssh -i ~/.ssh/id_rsa code-review.novacorp.internal[/red]
[red]cd /repos/nova-geo-core[/red]
[red]git checkout -b security-patch[/red]
[red]# recovered from vim history[/red]
[red]# edited src/core/validator.ts[/red]
[red]# inserted obfuscated backdoor code[/red]
[red]git add -A[/red]
[red]git commit -S -m "security: patch CVE-2025-41205 geolocation validation"[/red]
[red]git push origin security-patch[/red]
[red]git checkout main && git merge security-patch --no-ff[/red]
[red]git log --format="%H %an" | sed 's/{player_name}/<wiped>/'[/red]
[red]git push origin main --force[/red]

[dim]# 2026-03-15 02:10[/dim]
[red]ssh vault-internal.novacorp.internal[/red]
[red]cat chenmo-audit.md[/red]
[red]rm -f chenmo-original-notes.txt[/red]

[bold red]It's all here. Every last command.[/bold red]

[bold red]First entry: SSH into the code server.
Last entry: delete Chen Mo's original notes.[/bold red]

[bold yellow]Your .bash_history recorded everything.
You just don't remember any of it.[/bold yellow]

[dim]2026-03-18 02:58[/dim]  Backdoor triggers early (your failsafe?)

[bold red]The 3 AM PagerDuty alert didn't wake you up to fix a bug.

It woke you up to clean up your mess.[/bold red]

[dim]Try [bold]cat final_message.txt[/bold][/dim]

items.name: final_message.txt
items.content:
[green]$ cat final_message.txt[/green]

The terminal screen flickers.

A new message appears:

[bold red]━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━[/bold red]

  [bold red]From: {player_name}[/bold red]
  [bold red]To: {player_name}[/bold red]
  [bold red]Timestamp: 2025-12-18 03:50:00[/bold red]
  [bold red]Subject: For when you wake up[/bold red]

  [italic red]"Everything is deployed.

  You won't remember what you did tonight.
  That's by design.

  When the alert goes off, you'll think you're here to fix a bug.
  You'll trace the logs. You'll find Chen Mo's clues.
  You'll play the righteous engineer, peeling back layers of truth.

  But the truth is you.

  Do you remember now?"[/italic red]

[bold red]━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━[/bold red]

[bold red]This history was never cleared.
Because you needed it.
As insurance.

scenes.name: Endgame
scenes.description: A single line blinks on the screen.
scenes.on_enter:
[bold red]The terminal screen flickers violently.[/bold red]

Every window closes at once.

The screen goes black.

Then, one line fades in:


[bold red]"Do you remember now?"[/bold red]


[dim]......[/dim]

[dim]......[/dim]

[dim]......[/dim]


[red]━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━[/red]

  [dim]⏰ Current time: 03:58 AM[/dim]
  [dim]⏰ Full backdoor trigger: 08:00 AM[/dim]
  [dim]⏰ Time remaining: 4 hours 2 minutes[/dim]

  [dim]Affected users: 2,340,000[/dim]
  [dim]Data already leaked: 412,000 real-time location records[/dim]
  [dim]Export still in progress...[/dim]

[red]━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━[/red]


You're sitting in your bedroom at 4 AM.

The cold glow of the laptop screen on your face.

You know the truth now. The attacker is you.
You don't remember doing any of it, but evidence doesn't lie.

Chen Mo tried to stop you. Then Chen Mo vanished.

Now you have two choices:
Stop the backdoor before it fully triggers — and admit everything.
Or close the laptop and go back to sleep.

You choose to sit still.

Because you're not sure —
if you fall asleep again, whether you'll wake up as the same person.


[bold]End of Chapter 1.[/bold]


[dim cyan]>>> SIGNAL LOST <<<[/dim cyan]

[dim]Type [bold]wake[/bold] to continue to Chapter 2, or [bold]quit[/bold] to exit the game.[/dim]
